topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered, a dull, unwelcome sound against the silence of my cluttered living room. I dragged myself to the door, the afternoon light doing nothing to lift the heavy weight in my chest. There, nestled amongst the junk mail and bills, was a thick, cream-colored card. Unaddressed. Just a simple, cheerful script wishing someone a happy birthday. My jaw tightened as I picked it up, the paper feeling flimsy and utterly misplaced. Who thought this belonged here? I unfolded it slowly, my eyes scanning the blank front, a flicker of irritation sparking behind my gaze. It was a gesture, misplaced and presumptuous, and I felt a sharp, unpleasant tightening in my throat. I crumpled the card once, then twice, the sound of the paper protesting under my grip echoing my own internal dissatisfaction.

[story 2]

I stared at the mailbox as if it held a personal affront. Every day felt like an intrusion, and today, it was this unsolicited piece of paper. When I finally wrestled the heavy metal box open, the sight of the card made me physically flinch. It was bright, saccharine, and utterly devoid of any context. "Happy Birthday," the looping handwriting declared. I snatched it out, my fingers brushing against the smooth surface as if it might contaminate me. A low, tight sound escaped my lips—a mixture of disbelief and profound annoyance. Why? Who had the audacity to send something so utterly generic? I tossed it onto the nearest surface—a stack of unpaid invoices—with a decisive slap. It was just trash, an unnecessary imposition on my carefully ordered existence, and I didn't want to acknowledge its existence further.

[story 3]

The sound of the mail slot opening always seemed to signal a minor inconvenience, but this time, something snagged my attention. A card. Unsigned. It lay there, stark white against the dull grey of the entryway rug. I approached it with deliberate slowness, my posture stiffening as I registered the sheer randomness of it. My brow furrowed, a deep crease forming between my eyes. It was an invitation to engagement I absolutely did not wish to grant. I picked it up, the texture unexpectedly soft beneath my fingertips. A deep, rolling sigh escaped me, laced with a distinct air of exasperation. I held it up to the light, examining the simple sentiment as if dissecting a faulty mechanism. It was frivolous, an unnecessary display, and the entire situation grated on my nerves, making me want to sweep the entire area clean just to erase the evidence of its arrival.
